text: I would rather walk than take a taxi.
I love this city. So do I.
I don't like crowds. Neither do I.
In my opinion, the food is great. I agree.
The beach is the best part. I'm not sure about that.
So, what do you think of the city? In my opinion, the old town is the best part.
So do I! I love the little streets. But to be honest, I prefer the markets to the museums.
Really? I'm not sure about that. From my point of view, the museums are amazing.
That's a fair point. I think it depends on the day. Today I would rather walk around the market.
Fair enough. I don't want to argue on holiday!
Neither do I! Let's do the market today and the museum tomorrow.

So, what do you think of this city? In my opinion, it is one of the most beautiful places I have ever visited. I think the old town is amazing, and the food is fantastic too. Some people prefer the beach, but I would rather explore the museums and the little streets. I really believe a place is about its people, not just its sights. Do you agree? I find that travelers who talk to locals always have a better trip. That is just my point of view, of course.

For a long time, I was afraid to share my opinion in English. When someone asked what I thought, I just smiled and said, it is nice. But a real conversation needs more than that. So I learned a few simple phrases. Now I can say, in my opinion, or I am not sure I agree. I learned that disagreeing politely is not rude at all. It actually makes the conversation more interesting. You do not need to win the argument. You just need to say what you honestly think, and listen to the other person with respect.

First, Tânia's friend asks what she thinks of the trip. Then Tânia gives her opinion and says she loves the old town. Next, her friend agrees and adds that the food is wonderful. After that, they politely disagree about the best neighborhood. Finally, they agree to visit both places the next day.
In my opinion, this city is wonderful.
I would rather walk than take a taxi.
In my opinion, this is wonderful.
